The use of the term "cultural" in the context of education is not new. It has been widely discussed in academic circles for decades. The idea that culture plays a significant role in shaping learning processes is well established. However, the integration of cultural elements into educational practices remains a challenge in many contexts. This is particularly true in multicultural societies where diverse cultural backgrounds coexist. Educators must recognize that students bring with them not only their linguistic skills but also their cultural knowledge and experiences. These factors influence how they perceive, interpret, and engage with educational content. Therefore, effective teaching strategies must take into account the cultural dimensions of learning. This requires a shift from a one-size-fits-all approach to a more inclusive and responsive model that values diversity. By doing so, educational institutions can foster a more equitable and meaningful learning environment for all students.

The first step in the process is to identify the specific needs of the users and the context in which the software will be used.  
This involves analyzing the tasks that users need to perform, the environment in which they will operate, and any constraints that may affect software design.  
Once these factors are understood, the next step is to define the functional requirements of the software.  
These requirements specify what the software must do to meet user needs, including features, capabilities, and performance criteria.  
After establishing the functional requirements, the development team begins designing the software architecture.  
This includes selecting appropriate technologies, defining system components, and establishing how these components will interact.  
The design phase is followed by implementation, where developers write the code according to the design specifications.  
Testing is then conducted to ensure that the software functions correctly and meets all defined requirements.  
Finally, the software is deployed to the users, and ongoing maintenance is performed to address issues, improve performance, and adapt to changing needs.
